annotator S.report_text:
These campaigns utilized specially-crafted malicious Microsoft Word documents and PDF files, which were sent as e-mail attachments to various personnel in an attempt to infiltrate the targeted organizations. Adversaries may send spearphishing emails with a malicious attachment in an attempt to gain access to victim systems.

CosmicDuke would often embed PinchDuke so that, upon execution, CosmicDuke would write to disk and execute PinchDuke Adversaries may abuse command and script interpreters to execute commands, scripts, or binaries.

performing separate information gathering, data exfiltration and communication with a command and control (C&C) server Adversaries may search local system sources, such as file systems and configuration files or local databases, to find files of interest and sensitive data prior to Exfiltration.

performing separate information gathering, data exfiltration and communication with a command and control (C&C) server Adversaries may steal data by exfiltrating it over an existing command and control channel.

While the Dukes employed both hacked websites and purposely rented servers for their C&C infrastructure, the group rarely registered their own domain names, preferring instead to connect to their self- operated servers via IP addresses. Adversaries may buy, lease, rent, or obtain infrastructure that can be used during targeting.

While the Dukes employed both hacked websites and purposely rented servers for their C&C infrastructure, the group rarely registered their own domain names, preferring instead to connect to their self- operated servers via IP addresses. Adversaries may use an existing, legitimate external Web service as a means for relaying data to/from a compromised system.

a large grouping of domain names was registered by the Dukes in two batches Adversaries may acquire domains that can be used during targeting.

MiniDuke is centered on a simplistic backdoor component whose purpose is to enable the remote execution of commands on the compromised system. Adversaries may abuse command and script interpreters to execute commands, scripts, or binaries.

MiniDuke is centered on a simplistic backdoor component whose purpose is to enable the remote execution of commands on the compromised system. An adversary may use legitimate desktop support and remote access software to establish an interactive command and control channel to target systems within networks.

CozyDuke is a highly versatile, modular, malware "platform" whose functionality lies not in a single core component but in an array of modules that it may be instructed to download from its C&C server. These modules are used to selectively provide CozyDuke with just the functionality deemed necessary for the mission at hand. Adversaries may transfer tools or other files from an external system into a compromised environment.

even the earliest known GeminiDuke samples encrypted any strings that might have given away the malware's true nature. Adversaries may attempt to make an executable or file difficult to discover or analyze by encrypting, encoding, or otherwise obfuscating its contents on the system or in transit.

the MiniDuke campaigns from February 2013 employed spear-phishing emails with malicious PDF file attachments. These PDFs would attempt to silently infect the recipient with MiniDuke, while distracting them by displaying a decoy document. Adversaries may send spearphishing emails with a malicious attachment in an attempt to gain access to victim systems.

the MiniDuke campaigns from February 2013 employed spear-phishing emails with malicious PDF file attachments. These PDFs would attempt to silently infect the recipient with MiniDuke, while distracting them by displaying a decoy document. An adversary may rely upon a user opening a malicious file in order to gain execution.

password stealing, information gathering Adversaries may attempt to dump credentials to obtain account login and credential material, normally in the form of a hash or a clear text password.

denial of service (DoS) attacks Adversaries may perform Endpoint Denial of Service (DoS) attacks to degrade or block the availability of services to users.

This campaign, like later CozyDuke campaigns, began with spear-phishing emails that tried to impersonate commonly seen spam emails. These spear-phishing emails would contain links that eventually lead the victim to becoming infected with CozyDuke. Adversaries may send spearphishing emails with a malicious link in an attempt to gain access to victim systems.

a malicious Tor exit node Adversaries may compromise third-party infrastructure that can be used during targeting.

Executing the modified applications obtained this way would result in the victim being infected with unidentified malware. An adversary may rely upon specific actions by a user in order to gain execution.

one of these modules gathers system information An adversary may attempt to get detailed information about the operating system and hardware, including version, patches, hotfixes, service packs, and architecture.

another attempts to steal the victim's usernames and passwords Adversaries may attempt to dump credentials to obtain account login and credential material, normally in the form of a hash or a clear text password.

one is designed for use in DoS attacks Adversaries may perform Endpoint Denial of Service (DoS) attacks to degrade or block the availability of services to users.

thousands of recipients being sent spear-phishing emails that contained links to compromised websites hosting CozyDuke Adversaries may send spearphishing emails with a malicious link in an attempt to gain access to victim systems.

thousands of recipients being sent spear-phishing emails that contained links to compromised websites hosting CozyDuke Adversaries may compromise third-party infrastructure that can be used during targeting.

They would then use the toolset to gather initial information on the victims An adversary may attempt to get detailed information about the operating system and hardware, including version, patches, hotfixes, service packs, and architecture.

The simpler one will connect to a hardcoded C&C server over HTTP or HTTPS to download commands to execute. Adversaries may communicate using OSI application layer protocols to avoid detection/network filtering by blending in with existing traffic.

The more advanced variant, on the other hand, will use an algorithm to generate a periodically-changing Twitter account name and will then attempt to find tweets from that account containing links to the actual download location of the commands to execute. Adversaries may use an existing, legitimate external Web service as a means for relaying data to/from a compromised system.

Both backdoors (internally referred to by their authors as "BastionSolution" and "OneDriveSolution") essentially allow the operator to remotely execute commands on the compromised machine. Adversaries may abuse command and script interpreters to execute commands, scripts, or binaries.

the BastionSolution variant simply retrieves commands from a hard-coded C&C server controlled by the Dukes Adversaries may communicate using OSI application layer protocols to avoid detection/network filtering by blending in with existing traffic.

the OneDriveSolution utilizes Microsoft's OneDrive cloud storage service for communicating with its masters Adversaries may use an existing, legitimate external Web service as a means for relaying data to/from a compromised system.

spear- phishing emails containing malicious attachments Adversaries may send spearphishing emails with a malicious attachment in an attempt to gain access to victim systems.

The PinchDuke information stealer gathers system configuration information, steals user credentials, and collects user files from the compromised host transferring these via HTTP(S) to a C&C server. Adversaries may search local system sources, such as file systems and configuration files or local databases, to find files of interest and sensitive data prior to Exfiltration.

The PinchDuke information stealer gathers system configuration information, steals user credentials, and collects user files from the compromised host transferring these via HTTP(S) to a C&C server. Adversaries may attempt to dump credentials to obtain account login and credential material, normally in the form of a hash or a clear text password.

The PinchDuke information stealer gathers system configuration information, steals user credentials, and collects user files from the compromised host transferring these via HTTP(S) to a C&C server. Adversaries may communicate using OSI application layer protocols to avoid detection/network filtering by blending in with existing traffic.

Unlike CosmicDuke and PinchDuke, GeminiDuke primarily collects information on the victim computer's configuration. The collected details include: •Local user accounts •Network settings Internet proxy settings • Installed drivers • •Running processes •Programs previously executed by users •Programs and services configured to automatically run at startup •Values of environment variables •Files and folders present in any users home folder •Files and folders present in any users My Documents •Programs installed to the Program Files folder •Recently accessed files, folders and programs Adversaries may collect detailed information about the victim's system, such as local user accounts, network settings, installed drivers, environment variables, and recently accessed files.

Unlike CosmicDuke and PinchDuke, GeminiDuke primarily collects information on the victim computer's configuration. The collected details include: •Local user accounts •Network settings Internet proxy settings • Installed drivers • •Running processes •Programs previously executed by users •Programs and services configured to automatically run at startup •Values of environment variables •Files and folders present in any users home folder •Files and folders present in any users My Documents •Programs installed to the Program Files folder •Recently accessed files, folders and programs Adversaries may enumerate accounts to learn about user and administrator accounts.

The collected details include: •Local user accounts •Network settings Internet proxy settings • Installed drivers • •Running processes •Programs previously executed by users •Programs and services configured to automatically run at startup •Values of environment variables •Files and folders present in any users home folder •Files and folders present in any users My Documents •Programs installed to the Program Files folder •Recently accessed files, folders and programs Adversaries may look for details about the network configuration and settings.

Unlike CosmicDuke and PinchDuke, GeminiDuke primarily collects information on the victim computer's configuration. The collected details include: •Local user accounts •Network settings Internet proxy settings • Installed drivers • •Running processes •Programs previously executed by users •Programs and services configured to automatically run at startup •Values of environment variables •Files and folders present in any users home folder •Files and folders present in any users My Documents •Programs installed to the Program Files folder •Recently accessed files, folders and programs Adversaries may gather information about the running processes on the system.

Unlike CosmicDuke and PinchDuke, GeminiDuke primarily collects information on the victim computer's configuration. The collected details include: •Local user accounts •Network settings Internet proxy settings • Installed drivers • •Running processes •Programs previously executed by users •Programs and services configured to automatically run at startup •Values of environment variables •Files and folders present in any users home folder •Files and folders present in any users My Documents •Programs installed to the Program Files folder •Recently accessed files, folders and programs Adversaries may search for files, folders, and directories on local and remote systems.

Unlike CosmicDuke and PinchDuke, GeminiDuke primarily collects information on the victim computer's configuration. The collected details include: •Local user accounts •Network settings Internet proxy settings • Installed drivers • •Running processes •Programs previously executed by users •Programs and services configured to automatically run at startup •Values of environment variables •Files and folders present in any users home folder •Files and folders present in any users My Documents •Programs installed to the Program Files folder •Recently accessed files, folders and programs Adversaries may search local system sources, such as file systems and configuration files or local databases, to find files of interest and sensitive data prior to Exfiltration.

CosmicDuke's information stealing functionality includes: •Keylogging •Taking screenshots •Stealing clipboard contents •Stealing user files with file extensions that match a predefined list •Exporting the users cryptographic certificates including private keys •Collecting user credentials, including passwords, for a variety of popular chat and email programs as well as from web browsers Adversaries may use keylogging to capture user input, including passwords and other sensitive information.

CosmicDuke's information stealing functionality includes: •Keylogging •Taking screenshots •Stealing clipboard contents •Stealing user files with file extensions that match a predefined list •Exporting the users cryptographic certificates including private keys •Collecting user credentials, including passwords, for a variety of popular chat and email programs as well as from web browsers Adversaries may capture screenshots of the victim's desktop to gather information.

CosmicDuke's information stealing functionality includes: •Keylogging •Taking screenshots •Stealing clipboard contents •Stealing user files with file extensions that match a predefined list •Exporting the users cryptographic certificates including private keys •Collecting user credentials, including passwords, for a variety of popular chat and email programs as well as from web browsers Adversaries may capture data stored in the clipboard, potentially revealing sensitive information.

CosmicDuke's information stealing functionality includes: •Keylogging •Taking screenshots •Stealing clipboard contents •Stealing user files with file extensions that match a predefined list •Exporting the users cryptographic certificates including private keys •Collecting user credentials, including passwords, for a variety of popular chat and email programs as well as from web browsers Adversaries may search for and collect files from a local system, including those with specific file extensions.

CosmicDuke's information stealing functionality includes: •Keylogging •Taking screenshots •Stealing clipboard contents •Stealing user files with file extensions that match a predefined list •Exporting the users cryptographic certificates including private keys •Collecting user credentials, including passwords, for a variety of popular chat and email programs as well as from web browsers Adversaries may attempt to dump credentials to obtain account login and credential material, normally in the form of a hash or a clear text password.

CosmicDuke may use HTTP, HTTPS, FTP or WebDav to exfiltrate the collected data to a hardcoded C&C server. Adversaries may steal data by exfiltrating it over an existing command and control channel. Stolen data is encoded into the normal communications channel using the same protocol as command and control communications.

The Tor node would intercept any unencrypted executable files being downloaded and modify those executables by adding a malicious wrapper contained an embedded OnionDuke Adversaries may compromise third-party infrastructure that can be used during targeting.

The Tor node would intercept any unencrypted executable files being downloaded and modify those executables by adding a malicious wrapper contained an embedded OnionDuke An adversary may rely upon specific actions by a user in order to gain execution.

SeaDuke is a simple backdoor that focuses on executing commands retrieved from its C&C server, such as uploading and downloading files, executing system commands and evaluating additional Python code. Adversaries may transfer tools or other files from an external system into a compromised environment.

its occasional use of Twitter as a C&C communication channel Adversaries may use an existing, legitimate external Web service as a means for relaying data to/from a compromised system.

annotator L.report_text:
MiniDuke, CosmicDuke, OnionDuke, CozyDuke, CloudDuke, SeaDuke, HammerDuke, PinchDuke, and GeminiDuke

specially-crafted malicious Microsoft Word documents and PDF files, which were sent as e-mail attachments to various personnel in an attempt to infiltrate the targeted organizations Using specially crafted Microsoft Word documents and PDFs as malicious e-mail attachments to infiltrate target organizations.

CosmicDuke would write to disk and execute PinchDuke Creation and writing of a malicious executable to disk and its subsequent execution.

separate information gathering Collection of information on the compromised system.

data exfiltration and communication with a command and control (C&C) server Exfiltration of data through a command-and-control (C&C) channel.

MiniDuke is centered on a simplistic backdoor component whose purpose is to enable the remote execution of commands on the compromised system Executing remote commands on the compromised machine.

the MiniDuke campaigns from February 2013 employed spear-phishing emails with malicious PDF file attachments Use of spearphishing attachments to deliver malware.

password stealing, information gathering Collection of credentials through various techniques.

denial of service (DoS) attacks Use of DoS attacks to compromise or limit the availability of systems.

CozyDuke campaigns, began with spear-phishing emails that tried to impersonate commonly seen spam emails. These spear-phishing emails would contain links that eventually lead the victim to becoming infected with CozyDuke Use of links in spearphishing emails to induce victims to visit compromised websites or download malware.

the email instead contained a link to a zip-archive file named "Office Monkeys LOL Video.zip", which was hosted on the DropBox cloud storage service Using a spearphishing email containing a link to a zipper file hosted on a cloud storage service (DropBox).

They noted that this node appeared to be maliciously modifying any executables the compromised Tor node modifies executables downloaded over an HTTP connection to introduce malware or other malicious coding.

that were downloaded through it over a HTTP connection. Using HTTP connections to download executables.

Executing the modified applications obtained this way would result in the victim being infected with unidentified malware. Modified executables are executed by victims, resulting in the infection.

one of these modules gathers system information and another attempts to steal the victim's usernames and passwords One of the capabilities of OnionDuke is to collect system information and attempt to steal usernames and passwords of victims.

the other two known OnionDuke modules are quite the opposite; one is designed for use in DoS attacks One of OnionDuke's modules is designed to perform Denial of Service (DoS) attacks.

thousands of recipients being sent spear-phishing emails that contained links to compromised websites hosting CozyDuke 'sending spear-phishing e-mails containing malicious links leading victims to a compromised Web site hosting cozyduke.

SeaDuke

HammerDuke

The simpler one will connect to a hardcoded C&C server over HTTP or HTTPS to download commands to execute communication with a command and control (C&C) server via HTTP or HTTPS to download commands for execution.

The more advanced variant, on the other hand, will use an algorithm to generate a periodically-changing Twitter account name and will then attempt to find tweets from that account containing links to the actual download location of the commands to execute.

Both backdoors (internally referred to by their authors as "BastionSolution" and "OneDriveSolution") essentially allow the operator to remotely execute commands on the compromised machine Using remote services to execute commands on compromised machines.

The PinchDuke toolset consists of multiple loaders and a core information stealer trojan

The PinchDuke information stealer gathers system configuration information, steals user credentials, and collects user files from the compromised host transferring these via HTTP(S) to a C&C server Data collection activity by an attacker from a local system.

The PinchDuke information stealer gathers system configuration information, steals user credentials, and collects user files from the compromised host transferring these via HTTP(S) to a C&C server Using C&C (Command and Control) channels to exfiltrate stolen data from the compromised system.

The PinchDuke information stealer gathers system configuration information, steals user credentials, and collects user files from the compromised host transferring these via HTTP(S) to a C&C server Use of web protocols (HTTP/HTTPS) for communication with the C&C server and transfer of exfiltrated data.

PinchDuke will also search for files that have been created within a predefined timeframe and whose file extension is present in a predefined list Collection of data from the local system, as files created within a given period and with specific extensions.

The GeminiDuke toolset consists of a core information stealer, a loader and multiple persistencerelated components

GeminiDuke primarily collects information on the victim computer's configuration collection of system information about the configuration of the victim computer.

The CosmicDuke toolset is designed around a main information stealer component

Keylogging Keylogging, which is the capture of user input data

Taking screenshots Capturing screenshots of the desktop or specific windows.

Stealing clipboard contents Theft of the contents of the system clipboard.

Stealing user files with file extensions that match a predefined list Collection of data from the local system, including user files with specific extensions.

Collecting user credentials, including passwords, for a variety of popular chat and email programs as well as from web browsers Theft of credentials stored in web browsers.

CosmicDuke may use HTTP, HTTPS, FTP or WebDav to exfiltrate the collected data to a hardcoded C&C server Use of protocols other than the standard communication protocol to exfiltrate stolen data, such as HTTP, HTTPS, FTP, or WebDav

CosmicDuke may use HTTP, HTTPS, FTP or WebDav to exfiltrate the collected data to a hardcoded C&C server Use of a command and control channel to exfiltrate collected data to a C&C server.

The MiniDuke toolset consists of multiple downloader and backdoor components

it has also commonly been used in conjunction with CosmicDuke and PinchDuke

CozyDuke

Command execution module for executing arbitrary Windows Command Prompt commands Execution of arbitrary commands through the Windows Command Prompt or other script interpreters.

System information gathering module system information gathering, which could be exploited by the system information gathering module to gather details about the compromised system.

Screenshot module capture of screenshots of the desktop or specific windows, used by the screenshot module to collect visual information from the system.

these executables were self-extracting archive files containing common hacking tools, such as PSExec and Mimikatz, combined with script files that execute these tools. use of scripts to perform specific actions, such as executing hacking tools via script files included in self-extracting archives

The OnionDuke toolset includes at least a dropper, a loader, an information stealer trojan and multiple modular variants with associated modules

The Tor node would intercept any unencrypted executable files being downloaded and modify those executables by adding a malicious wrapper contained an embedded OnionDuke Use of techniques to mask the activity or malicious nature of the software. In the specific case of OnionDuke, the malicious wrapper added to executable files could be considered a form of masking to avoid detection

Once the victim finished downloading the file and executed it, the wrapper would infect the victim's computer with OnionDuke before executing the original legitimate executable. persuasion of the user to run a malicious file, which in the case of OnionDuke occurs when the user runs the modified file containing the malicious wrapper.

SeaDuke is a simple backdoor that focuses on executing commands retrieved from its C&C server, such as uploading and downloading files, executing system commands and evaluating additional Python code

HammerDuke is a simple backdoor that is apparently designed for similar use cases as SeaDuke

CloudDuke is a malware toolset known to consist of, at least, a downloader, a loader and two backdoor variants
